name: orm
context: ORM patterns and tools — Prisma, Drizzle, Sequelize, TypeORM, SQLAlchemy
info: Object-Relational Mappers (ORMs) bridge application code and databases by mapping objects/classes to database tables. This guide covers the two primary patterns — Active Record (model instances handle persistence) and Data Mapper (separate repository classes) — and the most widely used tools: Prisma (schema-first, TypeScript), Drizzle (SQL-like, TypeScript), Sequelize (Active Record, Node.js), TypeORM (decorator-based, TypeScript), and SQLAlchemy (Python, both Core and ORM layers). It includes when to use an ORM vs raw SQL, performance considerations, connection pooling, and N+1 query prevention.
triggers: connect my app to the database

sections.title: ORM patterns
sections.body: Active Record (Sequelize, Django ORM, Ruby on Rails) — model instances handle their own persistence via save(), create(), destroy(). Data Mapper (TypeORM in Data Mapper mode, SQLAlchemy, Prisma) — separate repository/client classes handle persistence, keeping domain objects pure. Active Record is simpler for CRUD-heavy apps. Data Mapper scales better for complex domain logic and testability.

sections.title: Prisma
sections.body: Schema-first: define models in `schema.prisma`, run `prisma generate` for type-safe client, `prisma migrate dev` for migrations (see [[database/migrations#tools-comparison]]). Auto-completes queries and relations. Handles connection pooling internally. Supports PostgreSQL, MySQL, SQLite, SQL Server, MongoDB, and CockroachDB. Use Prisma Accelerate for edge/serverless connection pooling (see [[database#connection-pooling]]).

sections.title: Drizzle
sections.body: TypeScript-native ORM with SQL-like syntax. Schema defined as TypeScript objects using `pgTable`, `mysqlTable`, or `sqliteTable`. Queries look like SQL: `db.select().from(users).where(eq(users.id, 1))`. Lightweight runtime, no code generation step. Drizzle Kit provides push (apply schema changes directly) and generate (create migration files). Best for teams that want type safety without leaving the SQL mental model.

sections.title: Python ORMs
sections.body: **SQLAlchemy** — two layers: Core (SQL expression language) and ORM (model mapping). Most flexible Python ORM. Supports Unit of Work pattern. Use with Alembic for migrations. **Django ORM** — tightly integrated with Django framework, Active Record pattern, built-in migrations. **Peewee** — lightweight, simple Active Record ORM for smaller projects. SQLAlchemy is the standard choice for non-Django Python applications.

sections.title: When to use an ORM
sections.body: Use an ORM for standard CRUD, rapid prototyping, and when type safety matters. Drop to raw SQL or a query builder for complex reporting queries, bulk operations, and performance-critical paths. Most applications use both — ORM for 90% of queries, raw SQL for the remaining 10%. Always check ORM-generated SQL with [[database/indexing#explain-analyze]] when queries are slow. Avoid N+1 queries by using eager loading (Prisma `include`, Sequelize `include`, SQLAlchemy `joinedload`).
